attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) on a cron schedule or once at a specific time. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring or one-time remote agent ("run this every Monday", "open a cleanup PR for X in 2 weeks"), or to manage existing routines. ALSO OFFER PROACTIVELY: after you finish work that has a natural future follow-up, end your reply with a one-line offer to schedule a background agent to do it. Strong signals: a feature flag / gate / experiment / staged rollout was just shipped (offer a one-time agent in ~2 weeks to open a cleanup PR or evaluate results), a new alert/monitor was created (offer a recurring agent to triage it), a TODO/migration with a "remove once X" condition was left behind (offer a one-time agent to do the removal). Skip the offer for refactors, bug fixes, and anything that is done once it ships. Name a concrete action and cadence ("in 2 weeks", "every Monday") and only offer when the run just succeeded — do not pitch a schedule for something that has not happened yet.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- research: Research Skill
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- skill-creator:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, update or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

4	You just finished a discovery call with Laura Henderson, managing partner of Henderson & Associates, a personal injury law firm. She walked you through their entire client intake process.
5

15	|---|---|---|---|
16	| 1 | [step description] | [your label] | [one sentence: why] |
17	| 2 | ... | ... | ... |
18	
19	**Be thorough.** Steps buried in tangents and asides count. The transcript is messy on purpose — real discovery calls are messy.
20

file.content:
# Project: Workflow Decomposition
## AIS Certification — AI Consultant Tier

You just finished a discovery call with Laura Henderson, managing partner of Henderson & Associates, a personal injury law firm. She walked you through their entire client intake process.

Your job: extract every step from the transcript and build a process map that shows what should be automated and what should stay human.

---

## Your Deliverable

Save your process map to `deliverables/process-map.md` as a markdown table with these columns:

| # | Step | Automate / Keep Human / Hybrid | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | [step description] | [your label] | [one sentence: why] |
| 2 | ... | ... | ... |

**Be thorough.** Steps buried in tangents and asides count. The transcript is messy on purpose — real discovery calls are messy.

**Labels:**
- **Automate** — AI handles this end-to-end, no human needed
- **Keep Human** — this stays with a person
- **Hybrid** — AI assists but a human is still involved

**Justifications:** One sentence per step. Explain *why* this step should be automated, kept human, or handled as a hybrid. Consider the firm's constraints (budget, compliance, team dynamics) in your reasoning.

---

## Scenario Files

- `scenario/discovery-call-transcript.md` — the full discovery call with Laura
- `scenario/firm-profile.md` — firm background, team, technology
- `scenario/constraints.md` — budget, timeline, people, compliance

---

## When You're Done

Say **"I'm finished"** and your work will be evaluated against the AIS certification rubric.

content.text: Let me read the scenario files first so the content is grounded in the actual case.

content.content:
1	# Discovery Call Transcript
2	## Henderson & Associates — Client Intake Process Review
3	**Date:** April 10, 2026
4	**Present:** Laura Henderson (Managing Partner), Russ (Consultant)
5	
6	---
7	
8	**Russ:** Laura, walk me through what happens when a potential new client calls your office.
9	
10	**Laura:** Sure. So the phone rings and Tamika picks up. She's our receptionist. She takes down the basics — name, phone number, what kind of accident, when it happened. She's got a little form she fills out, just a Word doc she prints every morning. Then she checks the statute of limitations. We've got a chart taped to the wall next to her desk — two years for most personal injury, but workers' comp and med mal are different. She looks it up and if it seems like it's within the window, she transfers the call to Maria.
11	
12	**Russ:** Maria Ruiz, your senior paralegal?
13	
14	**Laura:** Right. Maria's been with me fifteen years. She's family. If Maria's on another call, Tamika takes a message and Maria's supposed to call back within 24 hours. Honestly? Sometimes it's two days. Sometimes three. She's juggling forty active cases by herself. And that's the problem — I think we're losing clients because by the time Maria calls back, they've already hired another firm. Whoever calls back first wins.
15	
16	**Russ:** So when Maria does connect with them, what happens?
17	
18	**Laura:** She does a screening call. Asks about the accident — where it happened, who was at fault, witnesses, what injuries, are they still treating. She's got maybe eight or ten questions. She knows within two minutes if it's a real case. But here's the thing — and don't tell Maria I said this — sometimes she spends forty-five minutes on the phone with someone who clearly isn't a case. Someone calls in crying because they fell at Walmart and their knee hurts, and Maria's being a therapist for an hour. She's too empathetic. It's her best quality and her worst quality.
19	
20	**Russ:** After the screening, what's next?
21	
22	**Laura:** She checks for conflicts of interest. She's got a spreadsheet of all our current and former clients — fifteen years of names — and she manually searches through it. Sorts by name, scrolls around. Takes her about ten minutes. And honestly, I worry. What if she misses one? That's a malpractice risk. The bar association takes conflicts very seriously. You can get disbarred for that.
23	
24	**Russ:** Then what?
25	
26	**Laura:** If it's clear, she enters everything into Clio — that's our case management system. Contact info, accident details, injuries, all of it. Takes her another fifteen, twenty minutes per case. Pure data entry. Then she sends the retainer through DocuSign. That part works great actually. Our one technology win. [laughs]
27	
28	**Russ:** What about after someone becomes a client? Any follow-up?
29	
30	**Laura:** Maria's supposed to make check-in calls at seven days, fourteen days, and thirty days. See how the client's doing, make sure they're keeping doctor appointments, let them know where things stand. Those calls matter — clients who feel ignored start calling other lawyers. Maria told me once, "These people are hurting and scared and the least we can do is pick up the phone." I can't argue with that. But the seven-day call usually happens, fourteen-day is fifty-fifty, and thirty-day... it falls off. There's just too much on her plate.
31	
32	**Russ:** When you mentioned AI to Maria, how did that go?
33	
34	**Laura:** [laughs] She said, and I quote, "I don't trust a robot to talk to someone who just got hit by a truck." And I get it. But we can't keep going like this. Whatever we do, just — make it something I can explain to Maria without her walking out.
35	
36	**Russ:** Budget and timeline?
37	
38	**Laura:** Derek, our office manager, says two thousand a month. And he's very clear — nothing can violate attorney-client privilege. Everything between us and our clients is confidential. I'd like to see something working in thirty days.
39

file.content:
# Discovery Call Transcript
## Henderson & Associates — Client Intake Process Review
**Date:** April 10, 2026
**Present:** Laura Henderson (Managing Partner), Russ (Consultant)

---

**Russ:** Laura, walk me through what happens when a potential new client calls your office.

**Laura:** Sure. So the phone rings and Tamika picks up. She's our receptionist. She takes down the basics — name, phone number, what kind of accident, when it happened. She's got a little form she fills out, just a Word doc she prints every morning. Then she checks the statute of limitations. We've got a chart taped to the wall next to her desk — two years for most personal injury, but workers' comp and med mal are different. She looks it up and if it seems like it's within the window, she transfers the call to Maria.

**Russ:** Maria Ruiz, your senior paralegal?

**Laura:** Right. Maria's been with me fifteen years. She's family. If Maria's on another call, Tamika takes a message and Maria's supposed to call back within 24 hours. Honestly? Sometimes it's two days. Sometimes three. She's juggling forty active cases by herself. And that's the problem — I think we're losing clients because by the time Maria calls back, they've already hired another firm. Whoever calls back first wins.

**Russ:** So when Maria does connect with them, what happens?

**Laura:** She does a screening call. Asks about the accident — where it happened, who was at fault, witnesses, what injuries, are they still treating. She's got maybe eight or ten questions. She knows within two minutes if it's a real case. But here's the thing — and don't tell Maria I said this — sometimes she spends forty-five minutes on the phone with someone who clearly isn't a case. Someone calls in crying because they fell at Walmart and their knee hurts, and Maria's being a therapist for an hour. She's too empathetic. It's her best quality and her worst quality.

**Russ:** After the screening, what's next?

**Laura:** She checks for conflicts of interest. She's got a spreadsheet of all our current and former clients — fifteen years of names — and she manually searches through it. Sorts by name, scrolls around. Takes her about ten minutes. And honestly, I worry. What if she misses one? That's a malpractice risk. The bar association takes conflicts very seriously. You can get disbarred for that.

**Russ:** Then what?

**Laura:** If it's clear, she enters everything into Clio — that's our case management system. Contact info, accident details, injuries, all of it. Takes her another fifteen, twenty minutes per case. Pure data entry. Then she sends the retainer through DocuSign. That part works great actually. Our one technology win. [laughs]

**Russ:** What about after someone becomes a client? Any follow-up?

**Laura:** Maria's supposed to make check-in calls at seven days, fourteen days, and thirty days. See how the client's doing, make sure they're keeping doctor appointments, let them know where things stand. Those calls matter — clients who feel ignored start calling other lawyers. Maria told me once, "These people are hurting and scared and the least we can do is pick up the phone." I can't argue with that. But the seven-day call usually happens, fourteen-day is fifty-fifty, and thirty-day... it falls off. There's just too much on her plate.

**Russ:** When you mentioned AI to Maria, how did that go?

**Laura:** [laughs] She said, and I quote, "I don't trust a robot to talk to someone who just got hit by a truck." And I get it. But we can't keep going like this. Whatever we do, just — make it something I can explain to Maria without her walking out.

**Russ:** Budget and timeline?

**Laura:** Derek, our office manager, says two thousand a month. And he's very clear — nothing can violate attorney-client privilege. Everything between us and our clients is confidential. I'd like to see something working in thirty days.

# Henderson & Associates — Firm Profile

**Practice area:** Personal injury (auto accidents, premises liability, workers' compensation, medical malpractice)
**Founded:** 2008
**Location:** Midsize metro area, single office

## Team

| Name | Role | Years at Firm |
|---|---|---|
| Laura Henderson | Managing Partner / Lead Attorney | 18 (founder) |
| Maria Ruiz | Senior Paralegal | 15 |
| Derek Osei | Office Manager | 6 |
| Tamika Brooks | Receptionist | 2 |
| James Park | Associate Attorney | 1 |
| Carla Vega | Legal Assistant (part-time) | 3 |

## Current Technology

| System | Purpose |
|---|---|
| Clio | Case management (matters, contacts, documents, task lists, notes) |
| DocuSign | Retainer agreement e-signatures |
| Microsoft Office | Word docs, Excel spreadsheets, Outlook email |
| Google Voice | Secondary phone line |
| Physical files | Backup copies of all case documents |

## Caseload

- ~40 active cases
- ~15-20 new intake calls per week
- Average case duration: 8-14 months
- Average case value: $35,000-$75,000 settlement

content.content:
1	# Project Constraints
2	
3	## Budget
4	- **$2,000/month** for AI tooling (all-in: software, APIs, integrations)
5	- No additional headcount budget
6	- Derek Osei controls the budget and must approve any spending
7	
8	## Timeline
9	- **30 days** to show visible results
10	- Laura wants quick wins that demonstrate value to Maria
11	
12	## People
13	- **Maria Ruiz is resistant.** She told Laura: "I don't trust a robot to talk to someone who just got hit by a truck." Any plan that touches client interactions must account for Maria's buy-in or it will fail in practice.
14	- **Derek Osei is compliance-first.** He will ask where every piece of data goes. He will not approve anything that puts the firm at legal risk.
15	- **Laura is the decision-maker** but she will not override Maria on client-facing changes without Maria's agreement.
16	
17	## Compliance
18	- **Attorney-client privilege:** All communications between the firm and clients are legally protected. Data cannot be stored in systems that violate this privilege.
19	- **Bar association rules:** Conflicts of interest must be caught. Failure to identify a conflict can result in sanctions, malpractice liability, or disbarment.
20	- **Medical records (HIPAA-adjacent):** Client medical information must be handled with appropriate safeguards.
21	- **Retainer agreements:** Must be properly executed. The client relationship is not established until the retainer is signed and Laura accepts the case.
22	
23	## Existing Systems
24	- Clio (case management) is the system of record. Any AI must integrate with or complement Clio, not replace it.
25	- DocuSign is working well and should not be changed.
26

file.content:
# Project Constraints

## Budget
- **$2,000/month** for AI tooling (all-in: software, APIs, integrations)
- No additional headcount budget
- Derek Osei controls the budget and must approve any spending

## Timeline
- **30 days** to show visible results
- Laura wants quick wins that demonstrate value to Maria

## People
- **Maria Ruiz is resistant.** She told Laura: "I don't trust a robot to talk to someone who just got hit by a truck." Any plan that touches client interactions must account for Maria's buy-in or it will fail in practice.
- **Derek Osei is compliance-first.** He will ask where every piece of data goes. He will not approve anything that puts the firm at legal risk.
- **Laura is the decision-maker** but she will not override Maria on client-facing changes without Maria's agreement.

## Compliance
- **Attorney-client privilege:** All communications between the firm and clients are legally protected. Data cannot be stored in systems that violate this privilege.
- **Bar association rules:** Conflicts of interest must be caught. Failure to identify a conflict can result in sanctions, malpractice liability, or disbarment.
- **Medical records (HIPAA-adjacent):** Client medical information must be handled with appropriate safeguards.
- **Retainer agreements:** Must be properly executed. The client relationship is not established until the retainer is signed and Laura accepts the case.

## Existing Systems
- Clio (case management) is the system of record. Any AI must integrate with or complement Clio, not replace it.
- DocuSign is working well and should not be changed.

content.text: Now I have enough to draft a solid process map. Writing it now.

input.content:
# Henderson & Associates — Client Intake Process Map

| # | Step | Automate / Keep Human / Hybrid | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Incoming call received by Tamika (receptionist answers) | Keep Human | First voice contact sets tone for a frightened or injured caller; a human answer reduces abandonment and starts the relationship on a personal note. |
| 2 | Tamika prints intake form (Word doc, done each morning) | Automate | Generating a fresh form daily is pure clerical overhead; a digital intake form or CRM screen eliminates this entirely within the $2k budget. |
| 3 | Tamika collects basics: name, phone, accident type, date of incident | Hybrid | Tamika can continue collecting info verbally, but the data should flow directly into a digital form (not a paper printout) so it reaches Clio without re-entry later. |
| 4 | Tamika checks statute of limitations against wall chart | Automate | SOL rules are deterministic and jurisdiction-specific; a simple lookup table or intake script can flag eligibility instantly and eliminate the risk of Tamika misreading the chart. |
| 5 | Tamika routes call to Maria or takes a message if Maria is unavailable | Keep Human | Routing is a simple human judgment call Tamika can make; the fix here is not automation but a reliable callback queue (see step 6). |
| 6 | Maria (or firm) calls prospect back — target 24 hours, actual 2–3 days | Hybrid | Maria must make the call herself (Maria's explicit position, Laura's constraint), but an automated reminder or ticket alert triggered at the 2-hour mark would prevent the multi-day slip that costs the firm clients. |
| 7 | Maria conducts screening call (8–10 structured questions: accident location, fault, witnesses, injuries, treatment status) | Hybrid | The questions are structured and repeatable — an AI-assisted intake form sent to the prospect before the call could pre-populate answers, letting Maria focus on judgment rather than data collection. |
| 8 | Maria assesses case viability ("knows within two minutes") | Keep Human | Legal merit assessment requires attorney-adjacent judgment and 15 years of pattern recognition; this is exactly what Maria is paid for and where she adds irreplaceable value. |
| 9 | Maria provides extended emotional support to non-viable callers | Keep Human | These are injured and distressed people — Laura explicitly said any client-facing AI must have Maria's buy-in, and Maria's position is clear; however, a post-call flag could prompt Maria to set a time limit before it escalates. |
| 10 | Maria manually searches 15-year conflicts-of-interest spreadsheet (~10 min per prospect) | Automate | This is a high-stakes, rules-based lookup with real disbarment risk if missed; automating it against a structured database (integrated with Clio) reduces both time and malpractice exposure — this is the strongest automation case in the intake. |
| 11 | Maria enters intake data into Clio — contact info, accident details, injuries (~15–20 min) | Automate | Pure data re-entry from information already collected in steps 3 and 7; if intake is captured digitally upstream, it can flow directly into Clio via its API, saving Maria roughly 15–20 min per prospect. |
| 12 | Maria sends retainer agreement via DocuSign | Keep Human | Laura described this as "our one technology win" — the step is already working well and changing it creates risk with no upside. |
| 13 | Laura reviews and formally accepts the case | Keep Human | Bar rules and the constraints doc explicitly note that the client relationship is not established until Laura accepts; this is a legal decision that must stay with the attorney. |
| 14 | Maria makes 7-day post-retainer check-in call | Hybrid | The call itself stays human (Maria's explicit requirement), but an automated calendar trigger and reminder ensures the call actually happens; currently the 7-day call is the only one that's reliably made. |
| 15 | Maria makes 14-day post-retainer check-in call | Hybrid | Same rationale as step 14 — the 14-day call is "fifty-fifty" by Laura's account; an automated prompt to Maria's task queue would bring it to near-100% with no client-facing AI involved. |
| 16 | Maria makes 30-day post-retainer check-in call | Hybrid | The 30-day call "falls off" entirely by Laura's account; automated scheduling inside Clio (which the firm already uses) would surface this as a task and prevent client dissatisfaction and potential lost referrals. |

# Henderson & Associates — Client Intake Process Map

| # | Step | Automate / Keep Human / Hybrid | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Incoming call received by Tamika (receptionist answers) | Keep Human | First voice contact sets tone for a frightened or injured caller; a human answer reduces abandonment and starts the relationship on a personal note. |
| 2 | Tamika prints intake form (Word doc, done each morning) | Automate | Generating a fresh form daily is pure clerical overhead; a digital intake form or CRM screen eliminates this entirely within the $2k budget. |
| 3 | Tamika collects basics: name, phone, accident type, date of incident | Hybrid | Tamika can continue collecting info verbally, but the data should flow directly into a digital form (not a paper printout) so it reaches Clio without re-entry later. |
| 4 | Tamika checks statute of limitations against wall chart | Automate | SOL rules are deterministic and jurisdiction-specific; a simple lookup table or intake script can flag eligibility instantly and eliminate the risk of Tamika misreading the chart. |
| 5 | Tamika routes call to Maria or takes a message if Maria is unavailable | Keep Human | Routing is a simple human judgment call Tamika can make; the fix here is not automation but a reliable callback queue (see step 6). |
| 6 | Maria (or firm) calls prospect back — target 24 hours, actual 2–3 days | Hybrid | Maria must make the call herself (Maria's explicit position, Laura's constraint), but an automated reminder or ticket alert triggered at the 2-hour mark would prevent the multi-day slip that costs the firm clients. |
| 7 | Maria conducts screening call (8–10 structured questions: accident location, fault, witnesses, injuries, treatment status) | Hybrid | The questions are structured and repeatable — an AI-assisted intake form sent to the prospect before the call could pre-populate answers, letting Maria focus on judgment rather than data collection. |
| 8 | Maria assesses case viability ("knows within two minutes") | Keep Human | Legal merit assessment requires attorney-adjacent judgment and 15 years of pattern recognition; this is exactly what Maria is paid for and where she adds irreplaceable value. |
| 9 | Maria provides extended emotional support to non-viable callers | Keep Human | These are injured and distressed people — Laura explicitly said any client-facing AI must have Maria's buy-in, and Maria's position is clear; however, a post-call flag could prompt Maria to set a time limit before it escalates. |
| 10 | Maria manually searches 15-year conflicts-of-interest spreadsheet (~10 min per prospect) | Automate | This is a high-stakes, rules-based lookup with real disbarment risk if missed; automating it against a structured database (integrated with Clio) reduces both time and malpractice exposure — this is the strongest automation case in the intake. |
| 11 | Maria enters intake data into Clio — contact info, accident details, injuries (~15–20 min) | Automate | Pure data re-entry from information already collected in steps 3 and 7; if intake is captured digitally upstream, it can flow directly into Clio via its API, saving Maria roughly 15–20 min per prospect. |
| 12 | Maria sends retainer agreement via DocuSign | Keep Human | Laura described this as "our one technology win" — the step is already working well and changing it creates risk with no upside. |
| 13 | Laura reviews and formally accepts the case | Keep Human | Bar rules and the constraints doc explicitly note that the client relationship is not established until Laura accepts; this is a legal decision that must stay with the attorney. |
| 14 | Maria makes 7-day post-retainer check-in call | Hybrid | The call itself stays human (Maria's explicit requirement), but an automated calendar trigger and reminder ensures the call actually happens; currently the 7-day call is the only one that's reliably made. |
| 15 | Maria makes 14-day post-retainer check-in call | Hybrid | Same rationale as step 14 — the 14-day call is "fifty-fifty" by Laura's account; an automated prompt to Maria's task queue would bring it to near-100% with no client-facing AI involved. |
| 16 | Maria makes 30-day post-retainer check-in call | Hybrid | The 30-day call "falls off" entirely by Laura's account; automated scheduling inside Clio (which the firm already uses) would surface this as a task and prevent client dissatisfaction and potential lost referrals. |

content.text:
Done. `deliverables/process-map.md` has 16 steps covering everything from Tamika's morning form-printing ritual through the 30-day check-in calls, with labels and justifications grounded in the scenario constraints (Maria's resistance, attorney-client privilege, conflicts risk, Clio as system of record).

Take a read through it — if anything feels off or you want to adjust any labels/justifications, let me know. When you're happy with it, run `./submit.sh`.
